_comment: Position-anchor marker demo (Settings.show_position_anchor): a fresh scenario authored for THIS demo -- two 100-soldier Infantry lines (~8 ranks deep), not a copy of any existing demos/inputs file. Selects team 0's unit and issues an attack-move onto team 1's line (team 0 is stationary by default) so the two blocks close and grind through a prolonged melee. The cyan position-anchor dot (Unit._draw, gated by show_position_anchor) marks each unit's `position`, distinct from the whole-block soldier_summary.centroid -- watch the two separate slightly once contact happens. Per the CI-posted per-tick transcript for this scenario/seed (not a local run -- see .claude/memories/sparta-demos.md's note on verifying long-battle claims against CI's own transcript, not a local dump-state.sh): team 0's position TRAILS its own centroid through the violent opening of the clash (tick 400: behind by ~4.2 world units; tick 460: behind by ~5.7), since SoldierBodies.couple()'s bounded-velocity drift-correction lags a sudden, discontinuous centroid jump (current_speed reaches 80 on contact) rather than snapping to match it -- then pulls slightly ahead once the grind settles (tick 550: ahead by ~2.6). Team 1's shift is smaller still (within ~1 unit either way across the same ticks) -- the effect is real but subtle at this scale, not a dramatic visual leading-edge shift; the actual proof that the anchor now excludes a knocked-back/casualty-thinned deeper rank lives in test_couple_position_anchor_reaches_less_deep_than_the_old_selection_after_front_rank_casualties (test/unit/test_soldier_follow.gd), which isolates the effect from this clip's other confounds (charge-impact lag, mutual knockback). Requested via the session-only 'show_position_anchor' script field (Settings.set_show_position_anchor_session, never persisted). Timing: still closing (MOVING) through tick 370, contact and casualties begin by tick 400, and the grind continues through at least tick 550 -- so a single static, moderately-zoomed camera frame covers the whole approach-then-clash arc without needing a pan.
